data.title: Jensen Huang "defines" Nvidia: A converter that turns electrons into tokens
data.description: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang defined his company in one sentence: Input is electronics, output is tokens, and in the middle is Nvidia. On April 15th, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang gave an in-depth interview to podcast host Dwarkesh Patel, covering topics such as Nvidia's supply chain moat, the competitive threat of TPUs, and why Nvidia doesn't develop its own cloud services. Nvidia: "Electronics" — "Token" Converter Jensen Huang gave his most concise definition of Nvidia: Ultimately, something is needed to convert electrons into tokens. Converting electrons into tokens and making those tokens more valuable over time is difficult to fully commoditize. He further stated: "The input is electrons, the output is tokens, and Nvidia is in the middle. Our job is to accomplish this conversion as efficiently as possible." At the supply chain level, the focus is on the scale of Nvidia's procurement commitments—the latest financial report shows close to $100 billion, and industry analysis firm SemiAnalysis predicts this figure could reach $250 billion in the future. Jensen Huang explained the logic behind this: "If it reaches a trillion-dollar scale in the next few years, our supply chain can support it." He said this capability doesn't simply come from contracts, but from continuously "informing, incentivizing, and aligning" upstream CEOs—making them understand the scale and direction of the AI ​​industry, and then willing to invest in Nvidia's needs. CoWoS packaging is a typical example: two years ago it was the most scarce bottleneck in the entire industry, but after Nvidia's "several doubling" of production capacity, it's now basically no longer a topic of discussion. He judged that any bottleneck in the supply chain would not last more than two or three years: "EUV machines, logic capacity, packaging—these things are not difficult to replicate; all that's needed is a demand signal." He believes the real long-term constraint lies downstream: energy policy. "You can't build an industry without electricity; that's what takes time." (Image of TPU competition: Nvidia's computing stack offers the best price-performance ratio globally) Cloud giants (Hyperscalers) contribute about 60% of Nvidia's revenue, but major customers like Google, Amazon, and even OpenAI are increasing their investment in developing their own TPUs or other ASIC chips, directly questioning Nvidia's competitive position. Jensen Huang's response was two-fold. The first layer was a qualitative distinction: Nvidia is doing "accelerated computing," not dedicated tensor processing units. Accelerated computing covers almost all scientific computing fields, including molecular dynamics, fluid mechanics, data processing, and quantum computing, far broader than AI. We are the only company accelerating all types of applications. The second point, stated by Jensen Huang, is that no platform in the world can outperform Nvidia in terms of performance and total cost of ownership. Google's TPU and Amazon's Trainium cannot match it, and he does not agree with Trainium's claimed 40% cost advantage. Nvidia's gross margin can reach 70%, while ASIC chips have a gross margin of approximately 65%, meaning that companies cannot achieve significant cost savings by replacing products. Nvidia's computing stack offers the best price-performance ratio globally, without exception. No company can prove to me that any platform in the world today has a better total cost of ownership (TCO) for AI data centers than ours. No one. Dylan's InferenceMAX is there for everyone to use; without TPUs, there's no Trainium. Regarding the issue of Anthropic's extensive use of TPUs, Jensen Huang directly responded: Anthropic is a special case, not a trend. Without Anthropic, where would the growth of TPUs come from? It comes 100% from Anthropic. Without Anthropic, where would the growth of Trainium come from? It also comes 100% from Anthropic. He also admitted that his early failure to make a timely strategic investment in Anthropic was a misjudgment: "At the time, Google and Amazon AWS invested heavily, and Anthropic used their computing power. I didn't fully realize then that venture capitalists would never invest $5 billion or $10 billion in an AI lab. That was my mistake. But I won't make the same mistake again." Currently, Huang has made large-scale investments in OpenAI and Anthropic, reportedly amounting to $30 billion and $10 billion respectively. Why not build your own cloud? "Do as little as possible" is the philosophy. With a massive cash flow, Nvidia has recently been providing funding and computing power support to cloud service startups like CoreWeave. The market speculates whether Nvidia will bypass its customers and become a hyperscale cloud service provider itself, charging for computing power rentals. Jensen Huang's answer touches on the company's philosophy: "We should do as much as necessary as possible, and do as little as possible. The things we do, if we didn't do them, I truly believe they wouldn't have happened. But cloud services? If I don't do it, someone else will." He cited CoreWeave, Nscale, and Nebius as examples of "new cloud" companies—companies that would not exist without Nvidia's early investment and support. However, Nvidia's involvement was aimed at "enabling the ecosystem to thrive," not at transitioning to financial leasing or cloud computing operations. He also made a clear statement regarding "not picking winners": "When I invest in one, I invest in all of them." His reasoning was that Nvidia itself was almost universally considered "the least likely to survive" among 60 3D graphics companies at the time, and "I have enough humility to recognize that." Adhering to its "shovel seller" positioning, NVIDIA will never allocate GPUs on a "highest bidder wins" basis. Given the extreme imbalance between supply and demand, how does NVIDIA allocate its scarce GPUs? Jensen Huang explicitly denied market rumors of a "highest bidder wins" system. "We would never do that; it's terrible business practice. You set the price, and then people decide whether to buy." Huang explained NVIDIA's allocation logic: Prioritizing customer production forecasts and purchase orders (POs), then considering the readiness of customer data centers, and finally following a first-come, first-served principle. “I aspire to be a reliable cornerstone of the industry. If you place a $100 billion order for an AI factory, no problem, only we in the world can give you that kind of certainty.” Regarding the current chip export controls, Huang expressed a pragmatic view from the perspective of commercial and technical standards. He pointed out that computing power is only the underlying input of the AI ​​industry. When constrained, competitors can compensate for hardware gaps by stacking more energy, using more previous-generation chips, and optimizing algorithms. He stated that, first and foremost, it must be recognized that China does not lack chips. They have world-class computer scientists, and it's well known that a significant proportion of AI researchers in these AI labs are Chinese. They have about 50% of the world's AI researchers. They are competitors, and we hope the US wins, but I think dialogue and research exchange are probably the safest approach. Giving up the entire market won't allow the US to win the long-term technology race at the chip level and computing stack. That's a fact. NVIDIA's core competitive advantage is its mature developer ecosystem, with hundreds of millions of GPUs installed globally and comprehensive application scenarios. The industry's flywheel effect is clear, relying on the largest installed base, programmable architecture, rich ecosystem, and a massive number of AI companies worldwide to form a strong driving force for development. Furthermore, its leading position globally in cost-effectiveness, energy efficiency, and customer base further propels the flywheel of development. Huang Renxun stated that traditional Moore's Law provides approximately 25% performance growth annually. To achieve a 10 to 100-fold performance leap, a dual innovation in algorithms and computing architecture is necessary. The Blackwell architecture's energy efficiency is 50 times higher than the Hopper architecture; this achievement cannot be solely attributed to advancements in transistors. Architectural optimization and computer science innovation are the core keys. This architecture supports full-process programmability and full-stack collaborative design, relying on NVLink and Spectrum-X technologies; it would be impossible to complete without the CUDA ecosystem. Regarding the product roadmap and release schedule, Jensen Huang revealed that NVIDIA products will maintain stable annual iterations, from Vera Rubin to Vera Rubin Ultra, and then to Feynman, with new, consistently upgraded products released every year. Furthermore, we are the only company globally capable of undertaking AI computing power orders of any scale, ranging from $10 million to $100 billion.

data.title: Hayes: Empire, Straits, and Printing Presses: Three Fate Scripts for Bitcoin
data.description:
Source: Arthur Hayes, Founder of BitMex; Translated by: Jinse Finance Claw

>

Due to the lull in trading on Maelstrom in the first quarter, many brokers occasionally messaged me asking for my market views and what they could do for us. My consistent reply was: "It's a no-trade zone right now." Aside from slowly increasing our long positions on Hyperliquid, we essentially "did nothing" in the first quarter.

This interplay of two situations created a trading vacuum, at least for our long-term bullish positioning.

First, the surge in AI agents (i.e., "claws") will destroy the career prospects of ordinary knowledge workers in advanced Western economies with a flexible workforce (primarily Pax Americana), triggering a deflationary financial collapse. I detailed this in my article "This Is Fine" (see Jinse Finance's previous article "AI Triggers the US Financial Crisis"). Since that article was published, in an effort to turn Iran into the latest "trashcanistan," US President Donald J. Trump, with the support of his belligerent and down-on-his-luck "backing singer"—Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, has launched an unjust and selective war against Iran. Nearly seven weeks into the war, the only truly important question remains: what arrangements will be made regarding the flow of commodities and cargo through the Strait of Hormuz? Before offering my opinions on the war or geopolitics, I always state: I'm just a cryptocurrency enthusiast who loves skiing and two-steppinning. I know nothing about warfare, nor do I have any inside information about the intentions of global leaders. But I can decipher mainstream propaganda narratives and use my AI agent to perform simple mathematical calculations based on publicly available information. I try to strip away the noise and focus on what matters to my portfolio. Thankfully, I don't live in the Levant or the Middle East, so my life and freedom are not in danger. In my simple worldview, there are three scenarios worth considering; actually, there are four, but the fourth—nuclear apocalypse—is uninvestable, so writing about it is pointless. I will address each one individually and explore how they would affect the price of Bitcoin from a high-level perspective. I don't know the probability of each scenario, but what I want to know is: is there a way to construct a portfolio that, in the best-case scenario, absolutely outperforms the price of hydrocarbons (and their derivatives such as food and fuel), and in the worst-case scenario, even if it underperforms hydrocarbons, still outperforms all major asset classes? Scenario 1: Return to Normalcy In this scenario, the war ends immediately, and the pre-war status quo returns. However, the long-term trend of replacing expensive knowledge workers who manipulate digital symbols with cheaper, more efficient AI agents continues. The US economy is most affected because about 70% of its GDP is driven by consumer spending. Consumers use bank credit to finance their materialism, and these loans become assets on bank balance sheets. If the ability of ordinary knowledge workers to repay their debts evaporates, these banks will face functional bankruptcy, requiring central bank "clowns" to print massive amounts of money to bail them out. Scenario 2: Tehran Tollbooth In this scenario, the US military is unwilling or unable to prevent Iran from restricting ships from passing through the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has honored its promise to allow "friendly" vessels to pass through by paying a $2 million toll, payable in yuan, cryptocurrency, sanctioned dollars, or other diplomatic arrangements. The worst-case scenario for America's financial hegemony is that countries now have to raise yuan. Given that most countries have trade deficits with China, the only way to raise yuan on a large scale is to sell dollar assets (such as US Treasury bonds or large US tech stocks), buy physical gold, and then sell the gold through the Shanghai or Hong Kong gold markets for yuan. Among the world's top ten economies, only Brazil and Russia have trade surpluses with China, ranking only ninth and tenth respectively. In contrast, America's financial hegemony has the world's largest trade deficit, supported by an equally large capital account surplus. However, as countries sell dollar assets to raise yuan or fill commodity gaps in the spot market at extremely high prices, America's capital surplus must mathematically decline. The financialized US economy needs foreign capital to finance government spending; without it, the books don't match. Ultimately, falling bond prices (rising yields) and a stock market crash will force governments to provide funding by printing money. Scenario 2.5: US Blockade of the Strait of Hormuz An interesting turning point has emerged. After the US-Iran negotiations failed to reach a permanent ceasefire agreement, on Sunday, April 12, Trump announced that the US Navy would blockade all ships entering or leaving the Strait. Perhaps this blockade will evolve into a "bandit baron tollbooth," where ships must pay taxes twice, first to the Iranians and then to the Americans. Or, there may be too many ex post facto exemptions granted to various countries, making the blockade like a moldy piece of Swiss cheese. The above argument still holds true: if holding dollars doesn't guarantee that pirates won't bomb your ship, what's the point? Scenario 3: Imperial Re-bombing In this scenario, the US Air Force and Navy each play their part, destroying the Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps' (IRGC) ability to intercept shipping through punitive standoff bombing operations. The Strait reopens, and any ship can pass safely without additional fees. The restoration of imperial authority eliminates the need for countries to use any currency other than the dollar, and the need to scramble for expensive goods on the spot market—at least for a few days. The problem is that ending Iran's control of the Strait of Hormuz could very well mean the complete destruction of the entire country. Or, as Trump put it, "sending them back to the Stone Age." Many bloodthirsty Americans, raised with the propaganda that "Iran is the head of evil," will cheer this hardline stance. However, destroying Iran in this way means that before Iran takes its last breath, it will fulfill its promise, dragging down the entire Gulf region's commodity and energy production. Oil will never flow out again, and global central banks will have no choice but to frantically print money to rescue the global financial system in response to soaring commodity prices. If you live in some of these "rotten countries," your currency will experience hyperinflation against the dollar or ruble. The US and Russia will become the only remaining major regulators capable of filling the gaps left by the ruins of the Middle East. Famine and widespread social unrest will occur. Therefore, while your Bitcoin might be worth an infinite number of units of toilet paper fiat currency, your well-being will be seriously at risk if you don't escape in time. Before discussing Bitcoin's performance in various scenarios, let's provide some visual evidence for my argument using some charts. [Chart Analysis of Major Scenarios] [Return to Normalcy] I discussed this scenario in detail in "All Is Well," and here are some of the charts and tables from that time: [Image of chart] In short, the deflationary recession triggered by AI agents is as severe as the 2008 US subprime mortgage crisis. Consumer credit default rates are already rising, and the wave of large-scale layoffs hasn't even really begun yet.

Tehran Toll Station

Essentially, if this scenario occurs, it would mean the end of the petrodollar system and the rise of a new global reserve currency or a basket of currencies.

In the past five months, gold has been the largest export commodity of the United States for four months, surging 342% year-on-year.

They use dollars to buy gold and ship it out of the United States. This is the so-called American manufacturing revival; all that's left of the United States is a "barbaric relic." Sorry to all Trump supporters who were hoping to bring back high-paying factory jobs. Another presidential cycle, and blue-collar workers are once again being brutally exploited without any lubrication.

In the past five months, gold has been the largest export commodity of the United States for four months, surging 342% year-on-year.

They use dollars to buy gold and ship it out of the United States. This is the end of the so-called American manufacturing revival; all that's left of the United States is a "barbaric relic."

Sorry to all Trump supporters who were hoping to bring back high-paying factory jobs.

Another presidential cycle, and blue-collar workers are once again being brutally exploited without any lubrication. Step Two: Sell Gold for RMB [Images of gold and watermarknone are included.] alt="EgmQXs2UbiBydExldvb9XN87NddHn3DhsOfT54Td.png">

A Swiss refinery received gold from the United States and recast it into standard gold bars suitable for shipment to China.

Stars and Stripes block the Strait of Hormuz & Empire bombs again

To determine whether the strait is open or blocked, look at the above chart, or a similar chart generated by any market data software. The chart above shows the May 2026 WTI crude oil futures (CL1, white) and October contract (CL6, gold) prices. I chose WTI because this benchmark is most relevant to US gasoline consumers. Only if gasoline prices remain high before the November midterm elections will Trump substantially ease the situation. The lower part of the chart shows the price spread between the two contracts (far-month minus near-month), with the curve in a *spot premium (backwardation) state. Because the price increase in the far-month oil contract is less than that in the near-month contract, the market believes that oil circulation in the Straits will improve significantly. If this happens, a sharp drop in the near-month price will widen the spread. However, if the price increase in the far-month contract leads to a narrowing of the spread, the global economy will face catastrophic consequences. Forget about the war of words between Trump and the Iranian Revolutionary Guard; just focus on this chart. Currency Quantity vs. Currency Price After the outbreak of war, the two-year Treasury yield (white) surged above the effective federal funds rate (gold). This indicates that the market believes the Federal Reserve will raise interest rates to combat energy inflation. It's time to take sides. When pricing Bitcoin, do you think the quantity of money or the price of money is more important? I believe the quantity of money determines the price of Bitcoin. Bitcoin has no cash flow, so the discount rate derived from central bank policy rates is meaningless for valuing this magical internet currency. However, given that Bitcoin's supply is fixed, its fiat value depends on the total stock of fiat currency. Making this judgment is crucial because I think we may be entering a situation where major central banks, including the Federal Reserve, may be raising interest rates while simultaneously printing money directly or through the commercial banking system. As war drives up food and energy prices, capable governments will subsidize the prices of core economic inputs to avoid social unrest or famine. However, to prevent widespread inflation, central banks must suppress demand in credit-sensitive sectors by raising interest rates, thus destroying aggregate demand. Those who rely on borrowing for consumption will reduce spending when credit costs rise. If central banks only do this, my Bitcoin prediction is simple: Bitcoin prices will fall as people cut back on spending across the board, except for food and energy. But regardless of whether they are friend or foe to the American system, all countries must increase defense spending and stockpile key commodities. Do you want your country to be like Australia, which is almost 100% reliant on imports from China for refined hydrocarbons? China stopped exporting at the start of the war, while Australia had less than a month's worth of reserves. They had to beg Singapore and pay an inexorably high price for jet fuel, otherwise all those "country bumpkins" would be stuck on the Australian continent forever... I know some of you will gloat over this, especially Japanese skiers. Manufacturing bombs (especially nuclear bombs) to avoid becoming a junk-stan, coupled with stockpiling commodities, requires massive government borrowing. If domestic private investors are unwilling or unable to buy these junk bonds, central banks and/or commercial banks must print money to buy them, thereby expanding the fiat currency supply. The coexistence of rising central bank policy rates and an expanding money supply will lead to a divergence in the prices of risky assets. Assets valued based on discounted cash flow will plummet as currency prices rise. Assets with fixed or near-fixed supply, such as Bitcoin and gold, will rise, as central banks and commercial banks expand credit to finance wars and commodity stockpiles. Keep this logic in mind when reading my assessments of Bitcoin in various scenarios. You must judge the relative importance of quantity versus price in currency, otherwise you cannot understand the seemingly contradictory price movements of different risky assets. Bitcoin Outlook in Various Scenarios Return to Normalcy Bitcoin may see a slight rebound after the situation returns to pre-war levels. However, the AI-driven deflationary bomb is still ticking in the shadows. Unless the Federal Reserve provides liquidity to fill the black hole in bank balance sheets caused by consumer credit defaults, Bitcoin will not see a meaningful rise. This doesn't mean it can't reach $80,000-$90,000, but for me, to invest new fiat currency, I must wait for the Federal Reserve to signal a complete reversal of the alarm. I've already heavily invested in long positions because my strategy is purely long. I'm happy to see my account equity rise, but from a risk-reward perspective, now is not the time to significantly increase positions and push the portfolio to the highest risk level. I don't know how long it will take for the banking system to collapse. But every week I see news of massive layoffs at companies due to AI agents being far more efficient than ordinary humans, and news of rising consumer credit default rates. Here's an anecdote: I recently met a fellow entrepreneur who runs a successful crypto game company and is a veteran in the industry. We talked about the impact of AI on his business. He's a computer engineer by training, and during the 2025 Christmas holiday, he tried developing with the latest version of Claude and was amazed by its ability to quickly generate deployable code. So he held an offline meeting with his core engineers to discuss the impact of AI on the business over the next few months. He had them build an autonomous agent workflow, allowing AI to code 24/7. They automated code reviews, and every morning they woke up to usable, tested code waiting for senior engineers to review. One employee, with AI assistance, completed a roadmap that would normally take six months in just four days. After the meeting, my friend decided the company had to immediately adjust its workflow, resulting in 50% of the workforce facing extinction within the next few weeks. In the era of AI agents, ordinary engineers are worthless, while top talents will see their efficiency increase by 10 to 100 times with the help of AI. As models acquire more specialized domain knowledge, all mediocre knowledge workers face the risk of unemployment. Unfortunately, despite unemployment insurance, according to data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics and the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, the median annual maximum unemployment benefit in various U.S. states is approximately $28,000, far below the median wage of knowledge workers, which is $85,000 to $90,000. They have no choice but to default on consumer loans. The fiat fiat currency fractional-reserve banking system has reached its end. Bitcoin (yellow) / Gold vs. US Software SaaS ETF IGV (white) Even so, after the ceasefire, US SaaS software stocks resumed their decline, while Bitcoin held its ground and rebounded. This break in correlation is encouraging, but in my opinion, it's premature to declare that Bitcoin has ignored the deflationary pressures on knowledge workers caused by AI and to foreshadow a significant surge. As countries sell off dollar assets to raise yuan to pay tolls, US Treasury bonds and stocks will fall. This process may be slow because there are currently other payment options besides the yuan. However, given the leverage within the system, even a small snowflake could trigger a financial avalanche—selling triggers more selling, volatility soars, and markets freeze. At that point, monetary officials will have to intervene by printing money. A key indicator to watch is the MOVE index (US bond market volatility index). When this index breaks through 130, some form of money printing will occur. Bitcoin is unlikely to rebound significantly during periods of rising volatility and declines in large US tech stocks. Investors will sell Bitcoin to meet margin requirements as they seek to mitigate risk due to rising volatility and falling prices. Bitcoin will only rise when the situation deteriorates to a certain extent and a consensus forms in the market regarding the need for a bailout.

Just wait for Bessant or the next Fed Chair to press the printing press button.The risk-reward ratio of betting in advance is not worthwhile. I hope that Bitcoin can hold $60,000 when traditional finance collapses completely. If Bitcoin tests and holds this level a second time, I will generally be inclined to add to my position.

Stars and Stripes block the Strait of Hormuz & Empire bombs again

If the price of crude oil futures in the far month quickly catches up with the spot or near month price, the global economy will suffer a severe blow. Demand destruction will ultimately severely damage US Treasury bonds and US stocks. Similar to the previous scenario, Bitcoin's initial reaction is a decline. Once the highly leveraged Western financial system collapses, the printing press will run wildly. If the blockade ultimately ends with punitive bombing of Iran and the destruction of all energy production capacity in the Persian Gulf, it could lead to the destruction of the Iranian state. The Bitcoin rally driven by expectations of money printing may be short-lived, as the destruction of Iran would significantly increase the risk of World War III. Portfolio Construction As a pure long-only investor without leverage, Maelstrom can rely on time and compound interest to grow his portfolio. The slight outperformance of Bitcoin relative to IGV over the past few days has been encouraging, prompting me to reassess my pessimistic view of Bitcoin in the context of accelerating deflation among AI knowledge workers. Currently, the only assets I'm willing to add to are gold and HYPE (Hyperliquid governance token). The HIP-4 proposal will be launched in a few weeks, and I expect it to take a significant market share from Polymarket and Kalshi in the prediction market space. Besides this, all I can do every day is pray to Satoshi Nakamoto: that He might influence the minds of the world's political elites, leading them to swallow hallucinogenic drugs instead of dropping bombs.

data.title: Who can figure out the "Token Bill"?
data.description:
Author: Xiaojing, Tencent Technology

Computational power currency Tokens are reshaping the value coordinates of the AI ​​era, but while they are transparent, their value remains a black box.The AI ​​industry is still searching for a true anchor that can price results.

In March, Huang Renxun stood on the stage of GTC 2026 and described a new industrial era driven by tokens: AI factories continuously produce tokens, while agentic AI pushes the demand for inference to new peaks.

In March, Huang Renxun stood on the stage of GTC 2026 and described a new industrial era driven by tokens: AI factories continuously produce tokens, while agentic AI pushes the demand for inference to new peaks.

To put it simply, tokens seem like the most standard, unified, and quantifiable economic unit in the AI ​​era. Looking at the official pricing page of large models, we see a near-industry-standard uniformity: a clearly marked price per million tokens, with scales for input, output, caching, and batch processing. This high degree of uniformity in format often creates the illusion that the AI ​​industry has entered a competitive stage of mature rules and standardized products, with tokens being the most important metric of this era. However, the reality is quite the opposite. In April 2026, the input price of OpenAI's flagship model GPT-5.4 was $2.5 per million tokens, and the output price was $15, compared to $5 and $25 for Anthropic's flagship model Claude Opus 4.6, respectively. Judging solely from the listed prices, Anthropic was twice as expensive. However, for any enterprise technology leader, simply comparing these two numbers is meaningless. The differences between the two companies in context window strategies, tool call billing logic, and cache discount depth are enough to erase or even invert the price multiplier relationship. The price of a token is indeed transparent, but the "value" behind the token price is a black box. How much one must pay to obtain the same value is still difficult to define clearly. The pricing of a token itself is not mysterious. A developer who has long used APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) stated, "The price of a token is not a black box, and manufacturers cannot fake it. The number of tokens input and output can be estimated; even if the tokenizer is not public, it can be roughly calculated." [Image: Price comparison between GPT5.4 and Opus4.6] Economically, this is similar to a typical "homogeneous unit of measurement," like kilowatt-hours for electricity or gigabytes for storage. Tokens provide a standardized consumption metric, allowing buyers and sellers to trade on the same scale. The problem lies in the fact that tokens are not just traditional units of measurement; they measure intelligence and promise value. Users buy tokens to acquire the "intelligence" of the model, which can truly become a productive force. This includes writing runnable code, completing a customer service conversation, or producing data analysis. The exchange rate—how much capability each token can acquire—is the real black box. Furthermore, developers have reported that "sometimes, such as before a new model is released, the model vendor will reduce its intelligence and thinking intensity. This is a black box, but users can perceive it." In early April 2026, Stella Laurenzo, AMD's AI Strategy Director, released an analysis on GitHub based on 6852 Claude Code sessions. The data showed that starting in late February 2026, the inference depth of Claude Opus 4.6 decreased significantly—"reads-per-edit" (the number of file reads before each code edit) plummeted from 6.6 to 2.0, a decrease of approximately 67%. In other words, the model started modifying the code without carefully reading it. Laurenzo concluded: "When thinking becomes superficial, the model tends to take the lowest-cost action: modifying without reading, stopping before completion, shirking responsibility for errors, and choosing the simplest solution rather than the most correct one." On April 14th, media reports revealed another side of the story. Boris Cherny, the creator of Claude Code, responded that Opus 4.6 enabled "adaptive thinking" by default starting February 9th, and further adjusted the default effort level from high to medium (85%) on March 3rd. Anthropic believed this was "the best balance between intelligence, latency, and cost for most users." Users can manually type /effort high to restore full reasoning. The problem is: these changes were not communicated to users in any prominent way. Many developers only began to suspect that "the model has become stupid" after the code quality had clearly deteriorated. However, this is difficult to prove. A simple "inconsistent testing environment" perfectly suffices to address the doubts. "The probabilistic nature of large models makes it difficult to prove certain things." This is the most important and most difficult-to-price variable in token economics: consuming the same 1 million tokens can yield completely different inference quality. This varies between peak and off-peak periods, between default configurations and manually increasing effort, and between when subscribers are nearing their quota limits and when quotas are plentiful. The quantity and price of tokens are transparent, but how much "intelligence" is contained within them is unknown to users and cannot be predetermined. There's an economic concept called "hedonic adjustment," which states that when the quality of a product changes, even if the nominal price remains the same, the real price has changed. Tokens face this dilemma: the listed price and quantity remain the same, but the "value" may quietly diminish. This is more insidious and harder to trace than price increases.

II. The "Cache Hit Rate" that Affects Prices

Besides the black box of fluctuations in "intellectual content," there is a more hidden cost structure beneath the price list.

In February 2026, an update to Claude Code caused a significant drop in the cache hit rate of third-party platforms. This led to questions about whether Anthropic intentionally disrupted the caching of third-party models.

An engineer used AI tools to download the source code of 11 versions of Claude Code, from v2.1.0 to v2.1.41, and analyzed them one by one. The conclusion was: there was no deliberate logic in the code to disrupt third-party models.

However, starting with v2.1.23, Claude Code introduced a Claude-specific chunked caching mechanism. Optimizations like "cross-session global sharing and 1-hour validity" altered the structure of the system prompt. Third-party model APIs couldn't recognize these markers and could only rely on basic prefix matching. Prefixes, however, are highly unstable due to continuous changes in version numbers, build times, and A/B test variables. In simpler terms, Anthropic didn't intentionally "poison" the system, but in optimizing its own model's efficiency, it inadvertently disrupted the caching conditions that third-party models relied on. While not intentional, this incident highlights a crucial point: cache hit rate determines how much you pay for tokens. A developer's tracking of Claude Code usage data over a week showed that, under normal circumstances, 91% of tokens came from cache hits, with cache hit prices only one-tenth of the standard input price. If the cache were completely invalidated, input costs would skyrocket to 5.7 times the original cost. Boris Cherny, the creator of Claude Code, himself admitted: "When using a 1M context window, the cost of cache misses is very high. If you leave your computer for more than an hour and then resume an old session, you usually won't get a cache hit at all." There are also more noteworthy details. An analysis circulating in the community claims that Claude Code silently downgrades the cache duration from 1 hour to 5 minutes after detecting that a user has entered "Extra Usage" mode. In other words, if you stop for more than 5 minutes, a complete context rebuild will be triggered, and the cost will be deducted directly from the excess balance. According to media reports, in April, some Pro users reported that they could only post two hints on Claude Code within 5 hours. One user bluntly stated, "Until these caching bugs are fixed, any discussion about 5-minute or 1-hour TTL is pointless, because the numbers are completely wrong." The example of cache hit rate illustrates that even with the same result (value), the price paid can fluctuate wildly. III. The Budget Quagmire Behind the 300x Price Drop According to industry data, the price per token has fallen by approximately 300 times in three years, but companies' AI spending has become even more difficult to control. The reason is simple: the price drop couldn't keep up with the explosive growth in usage.

Figure: Over three years, the price of LLM Tokens has fallen by approximately 300 times—but this collapse in price has not made corporate AI spending more predictable. (Source: TokenCost)

Agent-based applications enable AI to autonomously execute complex, long-chain tasks, with a single transaction potentially costing dozens of times more than traditional dialogue.

... The six-person team at Branch8, an e-commerce technology company in the Asia-Pacific region, spent $2,400 in the first month after implementing Claude Code. After eight weeks of intensive optimization, including setting daily token quotas, limiting budget for thinking patterns, and switching non-critical tasks from Opus to Sonnet, they barely managed to reduce the cost to $680. Token spending management itself has become a job requiring specialized skills. At the Nutanix 2026 .NEXT conference, a CIO shared an even more extreme case: a developer incurred an unexpected $100,000 token bill, and had to explain it to an unprepared CFO—in his own words, it was an "extremely embarrassing meeting." Another attendee mentioned that some companies are already issuing daily token quotas to employees, "like a rationing system." A survey of 372 companies by Mavrik and Benchmarkit confirms this prevalence: 84% of companies reported that AI costs eroded their gross margins more than expected, and only 15% were able to keep budget errors within 10%. In economics, this is a classic example of "unit failure." When a unit of measurement cannot accurately reflect costs (such as implicit variables like caching and desensitization) or value, and the same token produces vastly different results in different scenarios, it loses its basic function as a measure and cannot help the market form consensus or reduce transaction friction. From another perspective, the surge in token consumption remains a core narrative for model companies. However, token growth is also a cost story. OpenAI's inference costs reached $8.4 billion in 2025 and are projected to rise to $14.1 billion in 2026, with total cash burn of approximately $17 billion. It has signed cloud infrastructure contracts worth over $500 billion. Anthropic has raised over $64 billion in total funding. Neither company is currently profitable. OpenAI raised $122 billion in April 2026 at a valuation of $852 billion, while Anthropic raised $30 billion in February at a valuation of $380 billion. Investors hope to see the computing power cost per token continue to decrease, making "selling tokens" a profitable business. However, Amazon initially sold standardized goods and cloud services, with a relatively stable unit economic model. AI companies sell tokens, something seemingly standardized but actually highly heterogeneous, and these heterogeneous components (intelligence content, caching efficiency, task adaptability) are precisely the core variables affecting cost and value. Whether the "scale effect" will materialize as expected is even more uncertain than in the e-commerce and cloud computing eras.

IV. The Industry is Searching for a Pricing "Anchor"

Returning to the core question: Who can accurately calculate the value of a token?

In the short term, no one can. The price of a token is transparent, but the intellectual quality it exchanges for fluctuates. The real cost behind it is layered by caching, framework design, and computing power efficiency, and the business value it generates varies depending on the scenario.

A unit of measurement bearing so many dimensions of uncertainty inherently indicates that it cannot yet become the standard of measurement for the AI ​​era.

Tokens have not become a commodity that can be priced in a standardized way. They are a temporary unit of accounting that everyone has to use before the AI ​​industry finds a value anchor.

Currently, the industry's pricing of tokens is essentially pricing the "right to use computing power"—buying the opportunity for the model to "think" for you. How deep or good that thinking is, and whether it ultimately solves your problem, is not within the scope of this price promise. The rationality of this pricing method is currently impossible for any single party to independently judge. Vendors cannot measure the business value of output for users, users cannot penetrate the model's reasoning process to determine whether each token is "worth its price," and investors see the consumption growth curve but not the value conversion rate of each token. Ultimately, what can truly determine the value of tokens may be discovering the "unit of output" that customers are willing to pay for, defining the true productivity of intelligence, and internally managing the conversion relationship between token costs and computing power costs to a predictable level.

data.title: Allbirds, the trendy sneaker brand that swept Silicon Valley, has also gone all-in on AI.
data.description:
Author: seed.eth, BitpushNews

Leonardo DiCaprio invested, Obama and Cook competed to wear it, Emma Watson actively endorsed it…

However, this "top middle-class brand" is not selling well.

On April 15, 2026, Allbirds, a popular American sneaker brand, announced a major development: the company would completely abandon its shoe manufacturing business and fully shift its focus to artificial intelligence computing infrastructure, renaming itself "NewBird AI". Following the announcement, the stock price surged from less than $3 to a high of over $24 during the day, a single-day increase of over 800%. Just two weeks ago, this once-star brand sold all its intellectual property and footwear assets for a paltry $39 million—a mere one percent of its peak market capitalization of $4.1 billion. From wool shoes worn by Silicon Valley elites to a shell company turning to tinkering with GPU computing power—Allbirds' story is more than just the rise and fall of a startup; it reveals the madness of today's capital markets: AI is a panacea. The Rise and Fall of the "Silicon Valley Miracle Shoes" In 2015, former New Zealand professional football player Tim Brown and renewable resources expert Joey Zwillinger founded Allbirds in San Francisco. Their vision was simple and clear: to create a comfortable shoe that didn't rely on petroleum-based raw materials, using natural materials such as merino wool and eucalyptus fiber. In 2016, their first product, the Wool Runner, was launched and quickly became a hit in Silicon Valley's tech circles. Google co-founder Larry Page, Apple CEO Tim Cook, and even former US President Barack Obama became fans of these wool shoes. Allbirds caught two excellent trends. First, the golden age of the DTC (Direct-to-Consumer) model—bypassing traditional retail channels, reaching consumers directly through the official website, and gaining complete user data and brand narrative control. Second, the ethical awakening of "sustainable consumption"—with environmental protection becoming a global consensus, a pair of shoes with a "zero carbon footprint" is itself a declaration of values. When these two narratives took root in the fertile ground of an economic upswing, Allbirds quickly grew from a Kickstarter crowdfunding project into a publicly traded company valued at over $4 billion. However, Allbirds' fall was almost as swift as its rise. Its business collapse followed a typical DTC (Direct-to-Consumer) script: a single blockbuster product supported the entire brand, and before its foundation was solid, it rushed into apparel and brick-and-mortar retail, resulting in an overextended strategy and a loss of brand focus. As more and more brands began to talk about environmental protection, and as competitors like Hoka and On surpassed it in performance and design, Allbirds' sustainability narrative was rapidly diluted. In 2022, the company's revenue reached a historical peak of $298 million; however, it subsequently declined steadily, falling to $152 million by 2025, nearly halved. Over the past five years, despite accumulating approximately $1.2 billion in sales, total losses reached a staggering $419 million. In 2024, the company received a delisting warning from Nasdaq due to its stock price remaining below $1 for 30 consecutive days, subsequently managing to maintain its listing status through a reverse stock split. In February 2026, Allbirds announced the closure of all its full-price retail stores in the United States. On March 30, 2026, Allbirds signed an agreement with brand management company American Exchange Group to sell its intellectual property and related assets for $39 million. The buyer owns brands such as Aerosoles and Ed Hardy and will continue to sell footwear products under the Allbirds name. The price has also been reduced. I checked the prices on the website today, and shoes that used to cost over $100 are now discounted to around $30... The listed company's "shell" awaits its next fate—and the answer came faster than anyone expected. From selling shoes to buying GPUs, the announcement on April 15th can be described as one of the most unexpected transformations in business history. Allbirds announced that it had reached a convertible bond financing agreement of up to $50 million with an institutional investor. The funds will be used to acquire high-performance GPU hardware and provide computing power access services to customers through a long-term leasing model. The company plans to change its name to "NewBird AI," with a long-term vision of becoming a "fully integrated GPU-as-a-Service (GPUaaS) and AI-native cloud solutions provider." The market reaction was almost frenzied. At the close of trading on April 14th, Allbirds' market capitalization was only about $21 million; after the announcement, the stock price briefly touched $24.31, and the market capitalization ballooned to approximately $165 million. On Fidelity Investments' trading activity list, Allbirds became one of the most actively traded stocks that day, demonstrating the enthusiasm of retail traders. This frenzied market reaction was less about pricing in NewBird AI's fundamentals and more about pricing in the "AI" label itself. Allbirds' transformation announcement revealed no specific information about customer resources, the technology team, or data center deployment plans—aside from $50 million in cash and a vague blueprint of "buying GPUs and then leasing them out." Independent consultant Bruce Winder commented, "I don't think Allbirds can bring anything substantial, except for brand awareness." It's worth noting that while shifting towards AI, Allbirds is also requesting shareholder approval in its SEC filings to amend its articles of incorporation to remove the phrase "serving the public interest"—meaning that this company, once proud of its B Corp certification, is actively shedding its environmental mission. From "saving the planet" to "selling computing power," Allbirds' value leap may be more symbolic than the business transformation itself. The AI ​​narrative remains the most powerful business magic. Allbirds is not the first, and certainly won't be the last. Over the past 18 months, from fast fashion to fresh food e-commerce, from logistics companies to home furnishing brands, a large number of traditional enterprises have rushed to label themselves as "AI." The reason is actually quite simple: the price-to-earnings ratio of shoe sellers is only slightly over 10, while that of computing power sellers can be inflated to over 50; GPUs are now a hard currency, more sought after than gold, and whoever has priority in delivery holds the key to resale profits; in addition, consumers' wallets are indeed thinner, so instead of continuing to spend money on advertising and competing with Temu for traffic, it's better to step back and gamble on enterprise-level AI computing power leasing—at least the story sounds better. Looking at history in a broader context, this kind of "changing clothes" is not new. During the crypto boom of 2017, a beverage company, Long Island Iced Tea, changed its name to "Long Blockchain Corp.", and its stock price surged by nearly 300% in one day, only to be delisted from Nasdaq the following year. In 2024, many Bitcoin mining companies shifted their focus to AI data centers, with Core Scientific being one of the most successful examples. From the dot-com bubble to blockchain, and now to AI, the capital market's script has never changed: the market is priced before profitability, and the narrative precedes reality. Allbirds' transformation essentially involved exchanging its remaining brand reputation and listed company status for a GPU procurement contract. The core issue is whether this entry ticket is truly valuable. AI infrastructure is a highly capital-intensive industry with extremely high technological barriers. The GPU leasing market already has players valued at billions of dollars, as well as deep involvement from massive cloud service providers like Amazon AWS and Microsoft Azure. Whether a company that once made shoes can survive in this crowded market with only $50 million in funding and a single GPU setup remains a huge unknown. Not to mention, this funding still needs approval at the special shareholder meeting on May 18th. Bloomberg industry analyst Poonam Goyal commented, "This move allows it to exit a structurally low-margin footwear and apparel business and enter a higher-value computing power business, but the execution risk remains high." We are witnessing a footnote to an era: any entity—whatever it was before—can be redefined as an AI company. As long as the story is compelling enough, capital will buy it. The AI ​​narrative remains the most powerful business magic today.

data.title: Will quantum computing soon crack Bitcoin's private keys?
data.description:
Author: Nian Nian's Friend, Cryptocurrency Research Expert

In March 2026, Google's quantum AI team, in collaboration with the Ethereum Foundation and Stanford University researchers, released a white paper detailing optimizations specifically for the secp256k1 elliptic curve used by Bitcoin and Ethereum. This reduced the required logical qubits from thousands to less than 1200, while another optimized path required approximately 1450 logical qubits. The quantum circuit built by the team could reverse engineer Bitcoin's private key in about 9 minutes. This news sent shockwaves through the cryptocurrency community.

Some self-media articles, such as "Shor's Algorithm Achieves a Historic Breakthrough: The Threshold for Quantum Computing to Crack Cryptographic Keys Drops Sharply," compiled related research and news reports, suggesting that quantum computing would soon crack Bitcoin's password, attracting widespread attention. However, this is not the case.

This article attempts to analyze the following: I. There is no risk that Bitcoin private keys will be cracked by quantum computers in the next two to three years. Considering the current state of technology in quantum computing, cryptography, and blockchain, the risk of Bitcoin private keys being cracked by quantum computers in the next two to three years is extremely low, essentially zero. While the online article is largely accurate in citing real research, it suffers from serious misinterpretations of the research conclusions and optimistic extrapolations regarding engineering implementation. Currently, all quantum computers (including the most advanced systems) are at least an order of magnitude larger than the fault-tolerant quantum computers required to crack elliptic curve cryptography, and several engineering hurdles remain to be overcome.

II. Verification of the Authenticity of Core Papers

The four core studies cited in the online article have all been verified to be authentic, as detailed in the table below:

All of the above studies were published between 2025 and 2026. Among them, the CRYPTO 2025 paper has undergone peer review and is a paper from a top international cryptography conference, so its authority is beyond doubt.

III. The Most Serious Conceptual Misunderstanding: Logical Quantum Bits ≠ Physical Quantum Bits

The most serious misleading aspect of the widely circulated article lies in its deliberate confusion between the concepts of logical quantum bits and physical quantum bits.

  • Logical Quantum Bits: "Virtual" quantum bits after quantum error correction encoding, are the actual computational units required by Shor's algorithm, and do not inherently contain physical noise.

  • Physical Quantum Bits: The actual physical units in quantum computer hardware, susceptible to environmental noise interference, requiring a large amount of redundancy to construct a single logical quantum bit.

The 1200 logical quantum bits proposed in the Google white paper, after error correction encoding, require approximately 500,000 physical quantum bits.

The 10,000 physical qubits proposed by the Oratomic team have a clock speed two to three orders of magnitude slower than the superconducting architecture, making it approximately 10 days to crack one ECC-256, thus failing to achieve the "9-minute transaction hijacking." An online article, by "multiplying and superimposing" parameters from different architectures, time windows, and preconditions, concludes that "tens of thousands of physical qubits are sufficient to crack the code"—in the parlance of StartBitcoin.org's analysis, this is "Frankensteinian mathematics," completely untenable in engineering reality.

IV. Current Technological Status: The Gap Remains Huge

Currently, the most advanced quantum processors only have a few hundred to a few thousand physical qubits, and their error rate is far higher than the fault tolerance level required to run Shor's algorithm. Currently, fault-tolerant neutral atom systems have only about 500 qubits, which is about 20 times less than the 10,000 qubits required in the paper.

... Furthermore, a key flaw in the "Chevignard scheme" mentioned in the online article has been deliberately omitted: while reducing the number of logical qubits, the scheme increases the number of Toffoli gate operations by more than 1000 times. This is equivalent to trading time for space, greatly increasing the requirements for the stability of the quantum computer. V. Core Logical Errors in the Online Article 1. Conceptual Confusion: Deliberately confusing "logical quantum bits" with "physical quantum bits," using the former's number to create panic. 2. Parameter Multiplication and Superposition: Multiplying the number of logical bits in the superconducting architecture with the error correction overhead of the neutral atom architecture to arrive at the erroneous conclusion of "tens of thousands of physical bits." 3. Selective Ignoring of Running Time: Although the neutral atom architecture requires fewer physical bits, it takes up to 10 days to crack, making it impossible to achieve "9-minute transaction hijacking." 4. Confusing Existence with Feasibility: The existence of a theoretical research result does not equate to engineering feasibility; the existence of an algorithm does not mean the hardware is ready. VI. Correctly Understanding the Boundary Conditions of "9 Minutes" The number "9 minutes to crack Bitcoin" is theoretically and physically valid, but its meaning is far narrower than suggested in online articles: These 9 minutes represent the time it takes for a quantum computer to complete the final calculation using only the exposed public key after all pre-calculations unrelated to the public key have been completed. This time is based on the assumption of microsecond-level gate operation speed and applies only to superconducting architectures. The time required to break the neutral atom architecture is measured in days. To achieve this theoretical capability, a fault-tolerant quantum computer with approximately 500,000 physical qubits needs to be built—currently, the most advanced superconducting quantum processors have only about 1,000 physical qubits. The key point is that the "9 minutes" is a description of a single operation assuming the machine already exists, not a prediction of when the machine can be built. The leap from 1,000 to 500,000 qubits represents a 500-fold increase in scale, not a gradual upgrade.

VII. Industry Authoritative Consensus: The Quantum Threat is a "Medium- to Long-Term System Upgrade Cycle," Not "Doomsday Within Two or Three Years"

There is a significant difference between the judgments of authoritative industry institutions and the doomsday narratives circulating online:

  • Bernstein (Wall Street Brokerage):: Quantum risk should be viewed as a "medium- to long-term system upgrade cycle," and the cryptocurrency industry has a three- to five-year window for post-quantum cryptography migration

  • Grayscale (Digital Asset Management Company):: Quantum risk is "real but controllable" for Bitcoin; technical solutions already exist; the real obstacle is community governance consensus

  • IX. Summary

    Core Judgment: Theoretical breakthroughs in quantum computing are progressing at an unexpectedly rapid pace, and this trend is real. However, "accelerated theoretical breakthroughs" do not equate to "attack tools being ready." From 1,000 physical qubits to 500,000, from laboratory demonstrations to large-scale fault-tolerant operation, quantum computing still faces fundamental challenges in three dimensions: physics, engineering, and cost. The real challenge facing the Bitcoin community is how to complete the system-level migration to quantum-resistant cryptography over a longer timeframe (approximately 5-10 years), rather than dealing with a non-existent quantum apocalypse within two or three years.

data.title: Warsh at the helm of the Federal Reserve: A crypto "insider" within a hawkish monetary framework.
data.description:
Shaw.AI, Jinse Finance

The U.S. Senate Banking Committee has announced that the nomination hearing for Kevin Warsh, the former Federal Reserve Governor and nominee for Chairman of the Federal Reserve, will be held on April 21, 2026. As a key candidate strongly promoted by the Trump administration, Warsh's financial disclosures and public statements have released key signals: he is not only a monetarist hawk, but also an "insider investor" deeply involved in the crypto and fintech sectors. Of his over $100 million in assets, crypto-related investments are deeply intertwined with Wall Street family offices, and his policy stance exhibits a complex characteristic of "prioritizing balance sheet reduction, supplementing with interest rate cuts, strictly regulating private blockchains, and promoting official blockchains." This article explores this from three dimensions: crypto asset correlation, monetary policy inclinations, and potential market impact, and answers core questions in the form of FAQs.

I. Deep Integration with Crypto Assets: An Intertwined Interest Between Investors and Policymakers Warsh's latest financial disclosures reveal that his personal assets far exceed $100 million. His investments in crypto and fintech span the entire chain, from infrastructure and application platforms to prediction markets, and are highly correlated with top Wall Street capital. 1. Crypto and Web3 Portfolio Overview (Disclosed) Tenderly: An Ethereum developer platform providing on-chain data, smart contract debugging and monitoring tools, and is a core infrastructure for DeFi and NFT development. Electric Capital Related Investments: Acting as a blockchain venture capital advisor, indirectly investing in multiple early-stage crypto projects. LemonCash: A crypto financial services platform covering crypto payments, custody, and wealth management services. Stashfin: A "new consumer lending bank" integrating traditional credit with digital asset collateral. Polymarket: A prediction market platform running on crypto tokens, a native Web3 application.

  • Institutional and Fund Layer

    • Duquesne Family Office: Two investments exceeding $50 million, forming the core source of its assets. This office has a long-term focus on crypto assets.

    • Bitwise, Basis: Early-stage investments in crypto index funds and algorithmic stablecoin projects.

    • Juggernaut Fund: Over $50 million in holdings, indirectly covering the crypto and AI sectors.

    2. Crypto Policy Stance: Three Shifts from Criticism to Pragmatism

    Wash's attitude towards crypto assets has not been static, but has clearly evolved with market maturity and his own changing identity:

    • Phase One (2018-2021): Critics called cryptocurrencies "software disguised as money," defining them as The second phase (2022-2024): Pragmatists changed their tune, calling Bitcoin a "sustainable store of value similar to gold," and acknowledging its function as a "policy watchdog"—Bitcoin price fluctuations would send market signals when monetary policy decisions were flawed. They also supported wholesale central bank digital currencies (CBDCs), believing they could improve the efficiency of dollar settlements and counter the digital yuan. The third phase (2025-present): Insiders publicly stated that "Bitcoin is not a substitute for the dollar, but it is an important asset," emphasizing the financial innovation value of blockchain technology. Their personal investments and policy stances formed a dual "interest-perception" bond. II. Monetary Policy Tendencies: A "Balance Sheet Reduction for Interest Rate Cuts" Framework of Overt Dovishness and Covert Hawkishness. Warsh's monetary policy is based on monetarism, with the core argument that "inflation is a policy choice," completely different from the easing path of the Powell era, exhibiting characteristics of "short-term hawkishness, medium-term dovishness, and structural tightening." 1. Core Policy Framework: Balance Sheet Reduction Prioritized, Interest Rate Cuts Secondary. Balance Sheet (QT): Aggressive Tightening. Warsh believes the Fed's $7 trillion balance sheet is the "root cause of market distortions and inflation," advocating for accelerated balance sheet reduction after taking office ($500-800 billion per month), with a long-term goal of reducing the balance sheet to below 10% of GDP (currently around 25%). Interest Rate Policy: Conditional easing supports interest rate cuts, but only on the premise of controlling inflation through balance sheet reduction—after the core PCE falls below 2.5%, a gradual interest rate cut of 25bp will be initiated each time, forming a unique combination of "interest rate cuts + balance sheet reduction in parallel." Functional Return: Streamlining the central bank's mission requires the Federal Reserve to divest itself of non-core functions such as climate and social equity, focusing on price and financial stability, clarifying the boundaries between fiscal and monetary policies, and opposing "fiscal-led" currency issuance.

    2. Key Differences from Traditional Hawks/Doves

    III. Potential Impact on the Crypto Market and Macro Assets

    Wash's dual identity as a "hawkish liquidity provider + crypto insider" will have a structural impact on the market, with short-term tightening and medium- to long-term divergence.

    Wash's dual identity as a "hawkish liquidity provider + crypto insider" will have a structural impact on the market, with short-term tightening and medium- to long-term divergence.

    1. Crypto Market: Short-term pressure, long-term benefits for compliance and blue-chip stocks. In the short term (6-12 months), accelerated balance sheet reduction will tighten global dollar liquidity, putting pressure on overvalued altcoins and DeFi leveraged products; Bitcoin's "digital gold" positioning limits its decline. In the medium to long term (1-3 years), regulatory clarity will favor "strict regulation of private coins and promotion of official blockchains," pushing crypto into the traditional financial regulatory framework, benefiting compliant platforms and blue-chip stocks such as Bitcoin and Ethereum. Liquidity Structure Optimization: After quantitative tightening squeezes out speculative bubbles, interest rate cuts will support risk assets, with crypto and US AI stocks forming a synergy. Accelerated Institutional Entry: Warsh's background in crypto investment will reduce policy uncertainty and encourage Wall Street institutions to increase their crypto allocations. 2. Macro Assets: The US dollar strengthens initially and then stabilizes, US stocks diverge, and US Treasury volatility intensifies. US Dollar: Short-term quantitative tightening pushes up the dollar index, while medium-term interest rate cuts coupled with a slowdown in quantitative tightening lead to a weakening dollar. US Stocks: Technology (AI) and financial stocks benefit from interest rate cuts and regulatory easing, while high-debt, low-profit growth stocks are under pressure. US Treasury Bonds: Balance sheet reduction and selling of long-term Treasury bonds lead to rising long-term interest rates; interest rate cuts lower short-term interest rates, steepening the yield curve. FAQ: Warsh, the Fed, and Cryptocurrency – Core Questions Answered Q1: Does Warsh's cryptocurrency investment involve a conflict of interest? Potential conflicts of interest exist, but they comply with US government ethics rules. His disclosed cryptocurrency assets are all early investments and indirect holdings; he does not directly hold Bitcoin or Ethereum in the spot market. During the hearing, he was required to promise to "avoid voting on cryptocurrency-related policies" or "divorce sensitive assets." However, as a deep investor, his policy inclination naturally leans towards "regulatory compliance and market institutionalization," rather than comprehensive suppression. Q2: Will Warsh support Bitcoin as a legal asset or strategic reserve? He won't directly support it, but it will indirectly benefit. He explicitly stated that "Bitcoin is not a substitute for the US dollar" and opposes its inclusion in the fiat currency system; however, he acknowledges its "store of value" function and supports the development of crypto assets within a regulatory framework, forming a pragmatic complement to Trump's "Bitcoin strategic reserve" proposal. Q3: Is the combination of "balance sheet reduction + interest rate cuts" good or bad for the crypto market? Short-term negative, long-term positive. In the short term, balance sheet reduction tightens liquidity, suppressing all risky assets (including crypto); in the medium term, with inflation falling, interest rate cuts starting, and regulatory clarity, **compliant crypto blue chips (Bitcoin, Ethereum, compliant platforms)** will see valuation repair, and leverage and altcoins will continue to be cleared out. Q4: What is Warsh's stance on central bank digital currencies (CBDCs)? Support wholesale CBDCs, oppose retail CBDCs. Advocate for CBDCs to be used only for central bank-interbank settlements, improving the efficiency of cross-border dollar payments and countering the digital yuan; oppose retail CBDCs for individuals, arguing they would impact commercial bank deposits and infringe on privacy—a stance consistent with some demands from the crypto community. Q5: What are the chances of Warsh's nomination being approved? Extremely high. Senate Banking Committee Chairman Tim Scott has clearly expressed his support, and committee-level approval is expected; in the full Senate vote, Republicans unanimously support him, plus some moderate Democrats, making confirmation virtually impossible. Q6: Will the Federal Reserve adjust its crypto asset regulatory policy after Warsh takes office? It will promote "regulatory consolidation." Advocating for the inclusion of crypto assets within the existing financial regulatory framework, with coordinated regulation by the Federal Reserve, SEC, and CFTC, clarifying Bitcoin as a commodity, stablecoins as monetary equivalents, and NFTs/tokens as securities, reducing regulatory arbitrage, and benefiting compliant institutions and blue-chip assets. Conclusion: The Era of the Federal Reserve's "Crypto Insider" Warsh's emergence marks the first time the Federal Reserve has been led by an insider deeply involved in the crypto and technology sector. His policies will present a combination of "strict monetary discipline, open digital finance, and a clear regulatory framework": on the one hand, aggressively reducing the balance sheet to repair the dollar's credibility; on the other hand, embracing crypto innovation with a pragmatic attitude, driving crypto assets from "fringe speculative goods" to "mainstream compliant asset classes." For the market, short-term pain will come from tightening liquidity, while in the medium to long term, it will usher in structural opportunities with clearer regulations and institutional entry.

data.title: Dollar depreciation and declining purchasing power
data.description:
Author: Anthony J. Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management

We can trace the root of many social problems back to the continuous depreciation of the US dollar over the past few years.

EJ Anthony of the Heritage Foundation recently pointed out: "Depending on the different inflation indices used, the cumulative inflation rate since January 2021 is between 23.6% and 27.1%." These are huge numbers, having a real negative impact on ordinary people. And this is only the officially released data. If we refer to the Truflation Index, its data shows that the compound inflation rate since January 2020 has approached 30%. For roughly half of Americans who hold all their assets in US dollars, this means their savings have suffered a devastating devaluation. Data from the Federal Reserve itself confirms this problem. Reports show that the M2 money supply has tripled since 2008, while the purchasing power of the dollar has declined by 38% over the same period. The savings of ordinary Americans are like ice melting under the scorching sun. But cumulative inflation and declining purchasing power don't tell the whole story. Over the past five years, nominal wages in the United States have risen considerably. There is more money circulating in the market, and some of that has indeed flowed into consumers' pockets. EJ Anthony further points out that, compared to January 2021, the purchasing power of the average American's weekly wage has decreased by 3%. Inflation has been widely reported, but wage growth has been largely overlooked. The problem is that inflation has second- and third-order effects, continuously eroding the economy and society. Housing affordability is a prime example. Home prices have been rising faster than wages, so we're watching as the home-buying ability of ordinary Americans steadily disappears. When people are worse off and left further behind, they begin to lose hope. When people lose hope, they become bolder risk-takers. Why not? What else do they have to lose? Nothing, really, isn't it? This is why we're seeing an explosive growth in sports betting, meme trading, and other high-risk investments. So what's the solution? There's a time-tested philosophy called the **Path to Success**. This path is very simple: **graduate high school, find a full-time job, and get married before having children.** If a person can do these three things, they can significantly reduce their chances of falling into economic collapse or poverty. This may seem simple to many of you, but we often overlook how many Americans end up on drastically different paths in life. I'm reminding you of this again because we are currently in the midst of a dollar devaluation crisis, while simultaneously witnessing a phenomenal bull market in commodities. According to Charlie Bilello data from Creative Planning: Sulfur: +67%; Aviation kerosene: +66%; Urea: +51%; Diesel: +50%; Heating oil: +40%; WTI crude oil: +37%; European natural gas: +34%. Gasoline: +32% Fertilizer: +31% Brent Crude Oil: +31% Coal: +14% Palm Oil: +10% Iron Ore: +7% Rice: +4% S&P 500 Index: +1% Volatility Index (VIX): -8% Commodity investors are reaping huge returns from this bull market, while ordinary Americans are suffering the negative consequences of the same price increases. The only real way to save the economy is through explosive growth led by the private sector and driven by technological innovation. We've already seen early signs of this growth in the artificial intelligence and robotics industries. We also need more sectors like aerospace, defense, and biotechnology to contribute to deflation and alleviate inflationary pressures. The US government will never stop printing money. Last month, we spent over $100 billion on interest alone on national debt—and that's just for one month. Therefore, our greatest hope lies in growth. Accelerate, accelerate, and accelerate again. It's possible. We just need to let entrepreneurs and tech talent take the lead.

data.title: A shoe company got involved in AI, and then what? Its stock price skyrocketed by nearly 600%.
data.description: A shoe company announced it's getting into AI computing power. Yesterday, its stock price surged nearly 600%. This is Allbirds, the brand known for its wool casual shoes. Overnight, it announced a "strategic transformation," aiming to enter the most crowded track: GPU computing power leasing. The story is always more interesting than the numbers. The market voted with its feet, with a sharp drop in after-hours trading. Capital is asking: How can a shoe company compete with Nvidia's customers for business? Meanwhile: PPG Industries rose over 6%, with solid performance and strong expectations. Bitcoin surged past $75,000, sending crypto stocks soaring. The Dow Jones Industrial Average dipped slightly, while the Nasdaq and S&P 500 hit new all-time highs. Who cares? At least tonight, everyone's attention is drawn to this "Cross-border Rhapsody". The core narrative of the capital market has never been "whether you did well or not", but "whether I believe your story". Allbirds told a perfect transformation story: from sluggish consumer goods to the sexiest infrastructure of the AI ​​era—computing power. Its stock price soared overnight. And then? The after-hours plunge was the first sobering slap in the face. This reveals the most authentic mentality of the current market: A thirst for narrative: still hungry for the story of "AI everything." Extreme sensitivity: zero tolerance for any logical flaws. Voting with money: buy if you believe, sell if you don't, without hesitation. What lessons can we learn from this? If you're starting a business, ask yourself: Is my business a "good story" or a "good fact"? If you're investing, be wary: Are you paying for a "vision" or for "cash flow"? Allbirds' attempt may succeed, or it may fail. That doesn't matter. Importantly, it reaffirms that ironclad rule: In the attention economy, narrative itself is an asset. But its value only exists when someone believes in it. The beginning of a story is always exciting. The ending is often a mess. Tonight, Allbirds used a stunning candlestick chart to tell the first half of the story. And the second half... I know this script all too well: The moment the news breaks, short-term funds flood in, driving the story to unprecedented heights; once things calm down, they realize, "Oh, you're still just a shoe seller," and walk away. A classic case of "the story ends and the party's over." Just enjoy it for fun; this kind of money is unattainable for ordinary people like us, and it's best not to try—it's too risky. Cryptocurrencies are celebrating, AI computing power is celebrating, and even shoe retailers are trying to jump on the bandwagon. But behind all this noise, companies quietly delivering solid financial reports continue operating at their own pace. This world needs dreamers, and it also needs bricklayers.

data.title: The US and Iran are gradually reaching a ceasefire agreement; the S&P 500 hits its first new high since the war began.
data.description:
Headline

▌US Media: US-Iran Negotiations Make Progress, Gradually Reaching a Framework Agreement to End the War

On April 15, according to AXIOS, two US officials stated that US and Iranian negotiators made progress in their talks on Tuesday, and the two sides are moving towards reaching a framework agreement to end the war. One US official stated that Trump's negotiating team (Vice President Vance, Middle East envoy Witkov, and Trump senior advisor Kushner) continues to speak with Iran and mediators by phone and exchange draft agreements, and is "close to the goal." A third official said, "We want to reach an agreement, and some people in Iran want to as well. The key is to get the entire Iranian government to agree." Sources said that if a framework agreement is reached, the ceasefire needs to be extended to negotiate details. "The details are complex and cannot be completed in two days," one official said. Another official stated that the US has not yet formally agreed to extend the ceasefire, and the two sides are still in continuous contact.

▌S&P 500 Hits First All-Time High Since Iran War

On April 16, the S&P 500 hit an all-time high during Wednesday's trading session, its first since the outbreak of the Iran war. Market expectations for a de-escalation of the conflict and strong earnings forecasts attracted investors back to risk assets. This new high amidst the ongoing geopolitical crisis signals a shift in market risk pricing, with traders appearing more willing to price in lower escalation risks in the short term. Last month, when the conflict erupted, stocks fell sharply, the oil market experienced a historic shock, and concerns about inflation and the outlook for US interest rates were reignited. Since the conflict began on February 28, the S&P 500 has fallen as much as 9%, but this did not meet the criteria for confirming a "technical correction," while the Nasdaq and Dow Jones both confirmed entering correction territory. The market was also supported by a positive corporate earnings outlook. Executives at major banks stated that despite the oil price shock, US consumers remain resilient, and there is a large backlog of mergers and acquisitions and IPOs.

In an interview, Trump stated that the possibility of reaching an agreement was "very high." On April 16, Iran's Tasnim News Agency, citing sources, reported on the 15th that after meeting with a delegation led by Pakistani Army Chief of Staff Munir, Iran will conduct a necessary assessment before deciding whether to hold the next round of negotiations with the United States. Sources indicated that the ceasefire between Israel and the Lebanese armed group Hezbollah could take effect "within this week," most likely after Israeli ground forces complete their capture of the southern Lebanese city of Bint Jubail. The conflict in Lebanon has become a sticking point in broader efforts to end the US-Israel war against Iraq, which began on February 28. Mossaleem said, "We are likely to see some transmission of oil prices to core inflation," and that by the end of the year, the underlying measure of price increases will be "slightly below 3%, perhaps around 3%," with the risk of further upside. Mossaleem said the Fed is likely to keep its policy rate in the current range of 3.50%-3.75% for "some time," while monitoring inflation, employment, and economic data in the coming months; many of his colleagues share this view. With rising oil prices and persistently high inflation rates across a range of service sectors, he would be open to raising interest rates if inflation begins to rise and potentially drive up inflation expectations. Musalaim also stated that the oil market represents the "third negative supply shock in 12 months," and coupled with rising tariffs and stricter immigration regulations, the inflation outlook and the job market face risks, potentially impacting economic growth. He believes economic growth this year will slow, but will remain between 1.5% and 2%. Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises?

Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been supported by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation).

Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," which can indeed function when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries are reduced, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but rather a completely new "next-generation gold."

Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.
